title: Utility
summary: 9708 AS utility — total, marginal, and diminishing marginal utility with demand derivation.
learningObjectives: Explain law of diminishing marginal utility.
Derive downward-sloping demand from utility maximisation.

simpleExplanation.title: Satisfaction from consumption
simpleExplanation.summary: Consumers allocate spending so marginal utility per pound is equal across goods, subject to budget.
steps: TU rises then levels; MU falls with extra units.
MU/P equal across goods at optimum.

flashcards.back: The total satisfaction gained from consuming a given quantity of a good.
The additional utility gained from consuming one more unit of a good (ΔTU / ΔQ).
As more units of a good are consumed, the extra utility from each additional unit eventually falls, ceteris paribus.
Consumers maximise utility when MU_x/P_x = MU_y/P_y for all goods — equal marginal utility per pound spent.
Water has high total utility (essential) but low marginal utility (abundant); diamonds have low TU but high MU (scarce) — explains price via MU not TU.
As quantity rises, MU falls (diminishing MU). Consumers will only buy more at a lower price, so MU/P stays equal across goods.

sections.content: **Utility** (7.1) underpins **consumer theory** in microeconomics. Link the **MU curve** to the **demand curve** and apply the **equi-marginal principle** in calculations.

**Total utility (TU)** measures overall satisfaction from consumption. It typically rises at a **decreasing rate** as more is consumed.

**Marginal utility (MU)** is the extra satisfaction from one more unit:

**MU = ΔTU / ΔQ**

The **law of diminishing marginal utility** states that MU eventually falls as consumption increases — the 10th slice of pizza adds less satisfaction than the 1st.

A rational consumer with a fixed budget allocates spending so that **marginal utility per pound** is equal across all goods:

**MU_x / P_x = MU_y / P_y**

If MU_x/P_x > MU_y/P_y, the consumer should buy more X and less Y until equality is restored. This is the **equi-marginal principle**.

Utility-maximising condition: $\frac{MU_x}{P_x} = \frac{MU_y}{P_y}$

Consumer buys more of a good until MU per pound equals that of all other goods.

sections.question:
A consumer has £12 to spend. Good X costs £2 per unit (MU: 20, 16, 12, 8) and Good Y costs £3 per unit (MU: 18, 15, 12, 9).

Find the utility-maximising combination.
sections.solution:
Calculate **MU/P** for each unit:

| Unit | MU_x/P_x | MU_y/P_y |
|------|----------|----------|
| 1st  | 20/2 = **10** | 18/3 = **6** |
| 2nd  | 16/2 = **8**  | 15/3 = **5** |
| 3rd  | 12/2 = **6**  | 12/3 = **4** |
| 4th  | 8/2 = **4**   | 9/3 = **3** |

**Optimal bundle:** Buy units with highest MU/P until budget exhausted.

- 1st X (MU/P = 10): spend £2, left £10
- 2nd X (MU/P = 8): spend £2, left £8
- 1st Y (MU/P = 6): spend £3, left £5
- 3rd X (MU/P = 6): spend £2, left £3
- 2nd Y (MU/P = 5): spend £3, left £0

**Optimum: 3X + 2Y** — check: MU_x/P_x = 6 = MU_y/P_y ✓

sections.content: As price of good X **falls**, MU_x/P_x rises relative to other goods → consumer buys **more X**. As quantity of X rises, **MU falls** (diminishing MU). The consumer only demands more X at lower prices — hence the **downward-sloping demand curve**.

For **diamond-water paradox** questions: price reflects **marginal utility** (utility of the next unit), not total utility. Water is cheap because its MU is low when abundant; diamonds are expensive because extra units are scarce and highly valued.

steps.caption: Marginal utility MU — extra utility from one more unit.
Equi-marginal principle: MU_x/P_x = MU_y/P_y at optimum.
Downward demand from falling MU as quantity rises.
